phenomenon: substitution toward cheap alternatives under price constraints
constructs: price elasticity age-based risk acquired-immunity demand
claim: When cost increases, agents select lower-cost substitutes; young populations show inelastic demand (access priority), older populations show elastic demand (price-sensitive).
Single women suppress signals of career ambition when male (especially unmarried male) peers will observe their stated preferences, despite identical private preferences.

phenomenon: political norm diffusion via diaspora contact
claim: Exposure to democratic institutions through migrant networks increases preference for democracy at home; effect depends on destination-country institutional quality, not on migrant selection.
Estimates of quantities are coded as gains or losses relative to a reference, not averaged across items; order of presentation dramatically biases final estimates.

phenomenon: heterogeneous pragmatic reasoning depths in population
claim: Population-level aggregate behavior masks individual differences in Theory-of-Mind depth; most speakers use level-1 reasoning but most listeners use level-1 exhaustive interpretation rather than level-2 pragmatic reasoning.

phenomenon: synergistic resource-scarcity effects on financial decision-making
constructs: liquidity criticality loss-framing lending-options tunnel-attention cognitive-bandwidth
claim: Multiple scarcity dimensions (cash-poor, critical need, loss-avoidance, no alternatives) compound attention tunneling and increase overborrowing beyond additive predictions; effect is strongest for repeat borrowers already in debt.
